sections.title: Inversions with Negative Adverbial Words and Phrases

content.subtitle: Use
items: hardly / scarcely / barely (... when)
no sooner (... than)
only in / only after / only when
not until
at no time / point / stage
in no way
little
never
not
not only (... but also/too)
on no account
rarely
seldom
under no circumstances

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Hardly had the flight landed when passengers started reaching for their phones.
Scarcely had the new semester begun when the students were handed their first assignment.
Barely had we stepped off the train when it began to pour with rain.
No sooner had the concert ended than the audience demanded an encore.
Only in exceptional circumstances should you access a patient's personal records.
Only after the specialist had confirmed the diagnosis did we begin to grasp the full seriousness.
Only when both sides reach a consensus will the negotiations be considered a success.
Not until the final results are published will we know who has won the championship.
At no time did the researcher claim that the findings were conclusive.
At no point did the researcher claim that the findings were conclusive.
At no stage did the researcher claim that the findings were conclusive.
In no way does this report imply that the company acted dishonestly.
Little did Hannah know that her colleagues had organised a farewell party.
Never have I witnessed such an extraordinary display of skill!
Not a single complaint did the hotel receive throughout the entire season.
Not only has the new policy cut waiting times but it has also greatly improved patient satisfaction.
Not only has the new policy cut waiting times but it has greatly improved patient satisfaction too.
On no account should you leave valuables visible inside your vehicle.
Rarely does the local council take residents' concerns seriously.
Seldom do recent graduates find their ideal job straight after finishing university.
Under no circumstances will the event organisers cancel proceedings due to light rain.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: With not until and only (when/after), invert the auxiliary and subject in the main clause.
examples: ✓ Not until the board reviews the evidence will the final decision be announced.
✓ Only when both teams agree on the terms can negotiations continue.

sections.title: Inversions with Adverbial Expressions of Place (+ verb of movement/position)

rows.label: here
items: Here comes the head of department now.

rows.label: there
items: There stood the competition winner, clutching her trophy.

rows.label: adverbial phrases
items: At the heart of the old city stands the ancient cathedral.
Opposite the railway station was a small but welcoming café.
In the basement of the archive building are stored thousands of historical documents.
On the steps of the gallery sat a group of art students, quietly sketching.

rows.label: participle phrases
items: Rushing through the terminal was a businessman with a laptop bag slung over one shoulder.

rows.label: in short answers
items: 'I've already seen that documentary.' 'Have you? So have I.'
'I don't think the new manager is particularly approachable.' 'No, neither do I.'

rows.label: after as, than, so and such
items: I find the new curriculum very demanding, as do most of my colleagues.
Students at this school perform better in the sciences than do pupils at neighbouring schools.
So complex is the immigration process that many applicants give up halfway through.
Such public enthusiasm was there for the new gallery that tickets sold out within hours.

rows.label: in conditional sentences
items: Were the chief executive to step down, it would create uncertainty throughout the whole company.
Should the chief executive step down, it would create uncertainty throughout the whole company.
Had I known about the transport disruptions, I would have left home far earlier.

rows.label: all (that)
items: All that Maya wanted was a little recognition for her hard work.

rows.label: it is/was ... who/which/that
items: It was Daniel who/that alerted the fire brigade.
It was I who submitted the complaint. (formal)
It was me that submitted the complaint. (informal)

rows.label: the ... thing
items: The first thing to do is compile a list of everything that has been reported missing.

rows.label: the day/time/period when/that
items: The summer when the prolonged drought struck was the worst in living memory.

rows.label: the person who/that
items: The person who designed the original suspension bridge was a young engineer from Bristol.

rows.label: the place where
items: The place where the historic signing ceremony took place was a grand eighteenth-century palace.

rows.label: the reason (why)
items: The reason (why) I chose this career was a deep desire to work directly with young people.

rows.label: the thing that
items: The thing that concerns me most is the complete lack of transparency.

rows.label: what
items: What concerns me most is the complete lack of transparency.
What Newton did was revolutionise our entire understanding of motion and gravity.
What happened was that a witness photographed the suspect leaving the building.

rows.label: so
items: Everything moved so quickly that I barely had a chance to take notes.
The queue had been moving so slowly that some people simply gave up and left.
It was so remarkable a performance that the entire audience rose to their feet.
There is so much background noise in this office that concentrating is almost impossible.

rows.label: such
items: The queue had been moving at such a slow pace that some people simply gave up and left.
It was such a remarkable performance that the entire audience rose to their feet.
There is such a lot of background noise in this office that concentrating is almost impossible.

rows.label: too
items: I had too little information available to make a reliable judgement.
The problem appears to be too complex for any straightforward solution.
The ambulance arrived too late to make any real difference to the outcome.

rows.label: enough
items: There simply aren't enough qualified nurses to cover all the shifts.
The candidate wasn't sufficiently confident to convince the interview panel.
She didn't speak loudly enough for the people sitting at the back to hear her.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: So and such can also be used without a that clause. Too is used for a meaning stronger than needed, often with a negative effect. Enough comes before nouns and after adjectives/adverbs.
examples: ✓ There's so much traffic on this road nowadays.
✗ The problem appears to be too complex for any straightforward solution to it.

sections.title: A: Original course practice
sections.instructions: Complete each original item using the target language from this lesson.

items.sentence: During the class project on Emphasis and Inversion, Mia wrote ...... to complete the model sentence.
items.answer: Hardly had I sat down at my desk when the phone started ringing.

items.sentence: In an original course practice item about Emphasis and Inversion, the best completion is ...... .
Sara checked the course practice notes and added ...... to the example.
items.answer: Barely had we unpacked our suitcases when the hotel fire alarm went off.

items.sentence: The online lesson included a short sentence about Emphasis and Inversion: the missing part is ...... .
items.answer: No sooner do the new safety regulations come into force than businesses find a way around them.

items.sentence: During the school club on Emphasis and Inversion, Nina wrote ...... to complete the model sentence.
items.answer: Scarcely had they presented their findings when the committee began raising objections.

items.sentence: In an original course practice item about Emphasis and Inversion, the best completion is ...... .
items.answer: Hardly had the new software been released when users started reporting serious problems.

items.sentence: Iris checked the course practice notes and added ...... to the example.
items.answer: It's true that no sooner had we ordered our food than the waiter informed us the kitchen was closing.

items.sentence: The language lab included a short sentence about Emphasis and Inversion: the missing part is ...... .
items.answer: Barely had the chairperson started to read the report when the audience began to murmur.

items.sentence: During the library task on Emphasis and Inversion, Lena wrote ...... to complete the model sentence.
items.answer: Hardly was the new road opened when a serious accident blocked both lanes.

items.sentence: In an original course practice item about Emphasis and Inversion, the best completion is ...... .
items.answer: Scarcely had Elena returned from her overseas trip when she was asked to travel again.

Mia began the class project before the lesson on Emphasis and Inversion. **ORGANISED**
The class project ...... before the lesson on Emphasis and Inversion.
